common_name: Painted Drop Tongue (Crinkle-Leaf Chinese Evergreen, Silver Queen Aglaonema)

planting.note: Grow in shade to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or a well-draining container mix; Aglaonema crispum is one of the parent species behind the thousands of hybrid 'Chinese Evergreen' cultivars sold in the houseplant trade. It is one of the most shade-tolerant of all ornamental plants, thriving in deep interior shade where virtually no other ornamental plant will survive. In zone 10-12, it can be grown as a permanent outdoor shade groundcover or low accent plant; in zone 9, it is a container plant that should be protected from temperatures below 50°F. CAUTION: all Aglaonema contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic to pets and irritating if the sap contacts skin.

directions: Grow in shade to deep shade in well-drained, humus-rich soil — Painted Drop Tongue is one of the most shade-tolerant ornamental plants available and will grow in the dim light of a north-facing room or the deep shade under dense tree canopy where nearly all other plants fail. Aglaonema crispum is a clumping, stemmed, tropical perennial in the Araceae family native to the Philippines and Southeast Asia — one of the approximately 25 species of Aglaonema, the group broadly marketed under the common name 'Chinese Evergreen,' and an important parent species in the development of the many hundreds of cultivated hybrids. The species form produces erect stems with large (12-inch), broadly ovate to elliptic leaves in dark, lustrous green with irregular blotches and streaks of silver-gray to pale green — a naturally variegated pattern that makes the leaves ornamental in the low-light conditions where the plant thrives. The crisped or wavy leaf margin (reflected in the species name crispum and some common names) adds texture. In bright indirect to low light, Aglaonema crispum produces consistent ornamental foliage year-round with minimal inputs. Occasional small aroid spathes (white spathe, white to green spadix) appear in mature plants but are not the ornamental feature. Grown as one of the most reliable and adaptable shade ornamentals available for indoor, patio, and outdoor shade garden use in zone 10-12. CAUTION: sap contains calcium oxalate; toxic to pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aglaonema species and hybrids for Florida shade gardens
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Aglaonema crispum species profile
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Aglaonema (toxic to cats and dogs)
University of Florida IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center — Shade ornamentals for zone 10-12

common_name: Voodoo Lily (Monarch of the East, Dead Horse Arum, Sacred Lily of India)

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring 2–4 inches deep in partial shade in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Typhonium venosum (formerly Sauromatum venosum) is remarkable for its ability to bloom without any soil at all — a dormant tuber placed on a plate at room temperature will produce its spathe and spadix unaided, drawing entirely on stored energy. The inflorescence produces a powerful, unpleasant odor for 1–2 days to attract fly pollinators. After this brief bloom, plant the tuber in soil where it will produce its ornamental, spotted, mottled foliage for the summer. In zone 9+, tubers overwinter in the ground and are very cold tolerant. Non-toxic external contact; ingestion causes irritation.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Voodoo Lily thrives in the same moist, shaded conditions as Amorphophallus and Arisaema and is one of the most cold-hardy of the large ornamental aroid genera, surviving winters well into zone 6 when the tuber is adequately mulched. Typhonium venosum (formerly Sauromatum venosum, often still sold as Sauromatum guttatum) is a tuberous, deciduous, perennial aroid native from East Africa through India, Southeast Asia, and China in the Araceae family — widely grown since Victorian times as a botanical curiosity for its ability to bloom from a bare, soil-less tuber. In spring, a single, exotic inflorescence emerges from the dormant tuber: a large, mottled, maroon-and-green spathe 12–18 inches tall surrounds an erect, dark purplish-maroon spadix with a long, whip-like terminal appendage (up to 12 inches). For 1–2 days, the inflorescence produces a powerful, fetid odor mimicking rotting flesh to attract the blowflies and carrion beetles that pollinate it — after which the odor dissipates entirely. Following this bloom, the tuber should be planted in soil, where it produces the handsome summer foliage: a single, large, dramatically divided, deeply lobed, mottled leaf on a spotted petiole that is quite ornamental in a shaded setting. A strange and fascinating botanical specimen with genuine garden presence in tropical and subtropical shade gardens. The tubers multiply steadily over time. Irritating if ingested; keep from pets and children.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Typhonium venosum (Sauromatum venosum) species profile
American Society for Horticultural Science — Sauromatum venosum: the Voodoo Lily
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental aroids for Florida gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Typhonium (irritant if ingested)

common_name: Shingle Plant (Silver Shingle Plant, Cryptantha Shingle Vine, Shingle Vine)

planting.note: Grow against rough bark, a moss pole, or a flat board in partial to deep shade with high humidity; Rhaphidophora cryptantha is one of the ultimate 'shingle plants' — it grows pressed completely flat against a surface, with its leaves arranged in overlapping, shingle-like rows, the entire leaf surface adhering to the substrate. In juvenile form (the only form usually cultivated), the leaves are small (1–3 inches), silvery-gray-green, and completely flat against the surface — the plant looks more like a lichen or bark overlay than a conventional plant. Very slow-growing. Requires warm (above 60°F), humid conditions. CAUTION: contains calcium oxalate; toxic to pets.

directions: Grow pressed against a rough bark surface or tree trunk in partial to deep shade with consistent warmth and humidity — Shingle Plant is one of the most unusual growing habits of any cultivated aroid and must be given a vertical, rough-barked surface to adhere to in order to develop its characteristic shingled growth form. Rhaphidophora cryptantha is a creeping, climbing, sympodial aroid in the Araceae family native to the rainforests of Papua New Guinea, a member of the large genus Rhaphidophora that includes several other popular climbing aroids. The plant exhibits one of the most extreme forms of the 'shingle plant' growth habit found in the Araceae: in juvenile form (which is the form commonly encountered in cultivation), the plant grows pressed entirely flat against a vertical surface, with each leaf arranged in an overlapping, shingled sequence along the climbing stem — the leaf blade, petiole, and stem all flattened against the surface as if laminated onto the bark. The leaves themselves are oval to oblong, 1–3 inches, in a silvery to gray-green color with subtle darker veining. The overall effect is of an intricate, overlapping mosaic of small metallic shields pressed against the substrate — genuinely unlike any other commonly grown plant in appearance. In mature, arboreal form (rarely achieved in cultivation), the leaves expand greatly in size and become more typical aroid leaf shape. The shingle plant growth habit is an evolutionary adaptation to the sheltered, low-light conditions of tropical rainforest tree trunks and buttresses. An extraordinary collector's plant for warm, humid, shaded environments. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Rhaphidophora cryptantha species profile
International Aroid Society — Rhaphidophora cryptantha: shingle plant cultivation
American Orchid Society — Climbing shade aroids for zone 10-12 gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Rhaphidophora (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Silver Cloud Philodendron (Quilted Silver Leaf Philodendron, Mamei Philodendron)

planting.note: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil or a chunky aroid mix; Philodendron mamei is a creeping, terrestrial-to-climbing philodendron that produces its most dramatic foliage in warm, humid, shaded conditions. The large leaves (12–24 inches) have a distinctly quilted, puckered surface texture between the lateral veins, with irregular silver-gray blotches on a dark green background — the 'Silver Cloud' pattern for which it is named. Grows best when allowed to creep and self-head along the ground or climb low onto a rough surface. Protect from temperatures below 55°F. CAUTION: all Philodendron parts contain calcium oxalate; toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in partial to deep shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil — Silver Cloud Philodendron produces its most dramatic, large-leaved, heavily silvered foliage in the warm, moist shade conditions of a protected tropical garden or sheltered indoor space in zone 10-12. Philodendron mamei is a crawling, self-heading to low-climbing, terrestrial philodendron in the Araceae family native to Ecuador and Peru, belonging to the diverse subgenus Meconostigma within Philodendron (the same group as Philodendron bipinnatifidum and P. selloum). Unlike the more familiar climbing philodendrons, P. mamei is primarily a creeping, ground-hugging plant that spreads by elongating stems close to the ground surface rather than aggressively climbing. The leaves are the plant's primary ornamental feature: large, broadly heart-shaped to ovate, 12–24 inches long, in dark, lustrous, forest-green with irregular silver-gray blotches scattered across the surface in a cloud-like, unpredictable pattern — the 'silver cloud' effect that gives the plant its popular name. The leaf surface between the lateral veins has a pronounced quilted, puckered texture, creating a tactile three-dimensionality unusual in philodendrons. The cataphylls (leaf sheaths) are a distinctive light green. Philodendron mamei grows moderately fast in warm conditions and remains manageable in size, making it appropriate for collectors' shade gardens, covered terraces, and shaded container plantings in zone 10-12. CAUTION: toxic to cats and dogs.
sources: International Aroid Society — Philodendron mamei: Silver Cloud cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron mamei species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Regal Anthurium (King Anthurium, Giant Velvet Anthurium, Royal Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in deep shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix with excellent aeration; Anthurium regale is one of the most spectacular of the large velvet anthuriums, producing leaves that can reach 36–48 inches or more in mature specimens, in deep forest-green with bold ivory-white primary veins creating a dramatic, architectural display. It requires consistently warm (above 60°F) temperatures, high humidity (60%+), and the well-aerated root medium typical of its epiphytic cloud forest origin. Slow-growing but very long-lived. Highly sought by collectors. CAUTION: calcium oxalate crystals present; toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in deep to partial shade in a well-aerated, humus-rich, chunky mix with consistently warm temperatures and high humidity — Regal Anthurium grows most magnificently in the cool, deeply shaded, perpetually humid conditions of its native Peruvian cloud forest habitat and rewards careful attention to these conditions with some of the most dramatic foliage of any cultivated plant. Anthurium regale is a large, terrestrial to epiphytic anthurium in the Araceae family native to the cloud forests of Peru (primarily the Department of Loreto) in the section Cardiolonchium — the velvet-leaved anthurium group that includes A. crystallinum, A. clarinervium, and A. magnificum. The plant produces enormous, pendant to spreading leaves: in mature specimens, individual leaves can reach 36–48 inches or more in length, are cordate (heart-shaped) at the base, and have a surface texture of deep, velvety forest-green with extraordinarily prominent ivory-white to cream primary veins (midrib and 3–5 main lateral veins) that stand out in brilliant relief against the dark background — creating an effect of dramatic, high-contrast architectural drawing in green and white. The back (abaxial) surface of the leaf is pale, matte green. The velvety upper surface is produced by papillate (bumped) epidermal cells that scatter light diffusely. Anthurium regale is among the largest-leaved and most imposing of the velvet anthuriums and is a prestige specimen for serious collectors of tropical aroids. Slow to reach full size but very long-lived. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium regale cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium regale species profile
American Orchid Society — Velvet anthuriums for tropical shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)
